summary: DeVault documents his complete departure from Google services, detailing the self-hosted alternatives he built or adopted over two years. He covers email (Postfix/Dovecot), YouTube consumption (RSS + mpv), music (a custom internet radio with friends), search (DuckDuckGo), file hosting (sr.ht), and a de-Googled Android setup with CyanogenMod and F-Droid. The post serves as both a practical guide and a demonstration that leaving Google is achievable, with each replacement offering more flexibility and customization than the original. He acknowledges collaborative document editing as the one remaining gap.
key_points: Self-hosted email, while the hardest to set up, provides the most side benefits: domain flexibility, sieve filtering, and full control over deliverability
YouTube can be consumed entirely outside the browser via RSS feeds piped to a desktop video player, eliminating ads and improving the experience
A communal self-hosted internet radio with friends, built on NFS-shared lossless music collections, surpasses any commercial streaming service
A de-Googled Android phone running CyanogenMod with F-Droid proves viable for daily use, replacing Google Play Services entirely
Each self-hosted solution offers more customization and flexibility than its Google equivalent, not less
money_quotes: I feel so much better about my privacy now that I've removed Google from the equation, and self hosting my things affords me a lot of flexibility and useful customizations.
It's so much easier to access YouTube this way than through the web browser - no ads, familiar keybindings, remote control support, and a no-nonsense feed of your videos.
This setup is way nicer than any commercial service I've used in the past.
I can be sending and receiving email from a new domain in less than 5 minutes now.
I recently patched Chromium for Android to support DuckDuckGo as a search engine.
I also keep a 128G flash drive on my keychain now that comes in handy all the time, and a big-ass file server on OVH.
key_insight: Replacing Google with self-hosted alternatives is not just feasible but often results in a more flexible, customizable, and satisfying experience than the commercial original.
